nodes.text: Sam's phone buzzes. The message is from her mother.
He went in. They're in the room together. Lawyer's there. I'm going to be okay. Eat lunch. Dad sends his love.
Sam reads it twice. She types back.
I love him too. Tell him.
I will, baby. Ramirez says he'll have him home by 5. We're having dinner together tonight. All three of us. He wants to.

Sam puts the phone face-down on the counter. She picks up the rag. She wipes the counter. A customer comes in — an older man, regular, wants two scratch-offs and a coffee. She rings him up. She hands him his coffee. He says, "Thanks, miss." He leaves.

She puts the rag down. She looks out the window at the parking lot. The white sedan is not there. The white sedan is, she now knows, ninety-eight miles west, at a NexCorp fueling station, doing whatever Claire is doing.

I do not know how this morning will end. I know my father is, right now, choosing the worse and harder thing. I know Diego is somewhere west of me being driven, at this moment, by a man whose name I do not know. I know the four of my friends are at their stations doing the small ordinary work of being teenagers in a Texas town on a Friday in late May.
The older system has, this morning, sent me to the Kwik Stop to be the visible safe daughter. The older system has a sense of irony.
